title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
A Picture Worth 10,000 Tests
Look at a \(p\)-value histogram from a gene expression study comparing two cancer subtypes:

The flat part (uniform across \([0,1]\)) represents null genes — no real signal
The spike near 0 represents true discoveries — genes that actually differ


You can see \(\pi_0\) (the proportion of true nulls) directly: it’s the height of the flat part.


This remarkably simple observation — that the histogram itself encodes the answer — is the foundation of everything in this lecture. But turning visual intuition into a rigorous procedure took decades.

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
Recap from Lecture 1

High-dimensional data: \(p \gg n\)
Multiple testing: \(m\) simultaneous tests generate many false positives
FWER (Bonferroni) is too conservative for discovery settings
FDR = \(E[V / (R \vee 1)]\) is the right error metric


This lecture: How to control FDR, and the deeper theory behind it.

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
The Benjamini–Hochberg (BH) Procedure
Given \(m\) hypothesis tests with \(p\)-values \(p_1, \ldots, p_m\):

Order the \(p\)-values: \(p_{(1)} \leq p_{(2)} \leq \cdots \leq p_{(m)}\)
Find the largest \(k\) such that \(p_{(k)} \leq \frac{k}{m} \alpha\)
Reject all \(H_{0(i)}\) for \(i = 1, \ldots, k\)


Theorem (Benjamini & Hochberg, 1995): Under independence, \(\text{FDR} \leq \frac{m_0}{m} \alpha \leq \alpha\).

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
BH: Geometric Interpretation
Plot \(p_{(i)}\) vs. \(i/m\). The BH procedure finds the rightmost point where the \(p\)-value curve dips below the line \(y = \alpha \cdot x\).


Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
m, m1 = 1000, 50
p_null = rng.uniform(size=m - m1)
p_alt = rng.beta(0.1, 1, size=m1)  # small p-values for alternatives
pvals_sim = np.sort(np.concatenate([p_null, p_alt]))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.scatter(np.arange(1, m + 1) / m, pvals_sim, s=3, color="black")
ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 0.10], color="red", linewidth=2, label="BH line (slope = α)")
ax.set_xlabel("i / m")
ax.set_ylabel("Ordered p-value")
ax.set_title("BH procedure (α = 0.10)")
ax.legend(loc="upper left")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
BH Under Dependence
The original BH result assumes independence of test statistics. In genomics, genes are often correlated.

Benjamini & Yekutieli (2001): Under arbitrary dependence, replace \(\alpha\) with \(\alpha / \sum_{i=1}^m 1/i \approx \alpha / \ln(m)\).


But: Under positive regression dependency on a subset (PRDS)—a condition often satisfied in practice—the original BH procedure still controls FDR at level \(\alpha\).

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
Stein’s Paradox: Borrowing Strength
In 1956, Charles Stein proved something that stunned the statistics world:
The sample mean is inadmissible for estimating 3 or more means simultaneously.

Efron & Morris (1977) made this concrete with baseball. To predict a batter’s end-of-season average, you do better by shrinking each player’s early-season average toward the grand mean of all players — even though the players have nothing to do with each other.


The James-Stein estimator (James & Stein, 1961): \(\hat{\theta}_i^{JS} = \bar{y} + \left(1 - \frac{(d-3)\sigma^2}{\sum_j (y_j - \bar{y})^2}\right)(y_i - \bar{y})\)
where \(d \geq 4\) is the number of means being estimated.


This is the founding insight of empirical Bayes: the other observations tell you something about the observation you are looking at. Efron generalized the idea — from baseball averages to 10,000 genes.

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
From Baseball to Genes
In classical statistics, you test each gene in isolation. Gene #4,237 knows nothing about genes #1–4,236.

Efron realized: when you test 10,000 genes simultaneously, the other 9,999 results tell you something about the gene you’re looking at — just as the other batters’ averages improve your estimate of any single batter.


If most genes show no signal, then the prior probability that any particular gene is non-null is low. The data itself reveals the prior — without you having to specify one.



“Letting the data determine the prior turns Bayesian inference from a subjective into a largely objective technique.” — Efron, Large-Scale Inference, Ch. 1

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
Storey’s Approach: Estimating \(\pi_0\)
BH controls FDR at level \(\frac{m_0}{m}\alpha\), which is conservative when \(m_0 < m\).
Storey (2002): Estimate \(\pi_0 = m_0/m\) and use an adaptive threshold.
For a tuning parameter \(\lambda\) (e.g., \(\lambda = 0.5\)):
\[\hat{\pi}_0(\lambda) = \frac{\#\{p_i > \lambda\}}{m(1 - \lambda)}\]

Intuition: \(p\)-values above \(\lambda\) are mostly from true nulls (which are uniform). Count them.
The R package qvalue implements this with automatic \(\lambda\) selection.

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
The \(q\)-value
Definition (Storey, 2003): The \(q\)-value of test \(i\) is the minimum FDR at which test \(i\) would be called significant.
\[q_i = \min_{t \geq p_i} \widehat{\text{FDR}}(t)\]

Analogous to a \(p\)-value, but for FDR instead of Type I error:

\(p\)-value: “If I reject this one test, what’s the false positive probability?”
\(q\)-value: “If I reject all tests at least this significant, what fraction are false positives?”

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
Efron’s Two-Groups Model
A more foundational perspective (Efron, 2004, JASA; developed fully in Efron, 2008, Statistical Science; and Efron, 2010, Large-Scale Inference, Ch. 2–4).
Each of the \(m\) cases belongs to one of two groups:

Null group (proportion \(\pi_0\)): \(z_i \sim f_0(z)\), typically \(N(0,1)\)
Non-null group (proportion \(1 - \pi_0\)): \(z_i \sim f_1(z)\)

The mixture density of the observed \(z\)-scores is:
\[f(z) = \pi_0 f_0(z) + (1 - \pi_0) f_1(z)\]

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
Estimating the Mixture
Key idea
We observe \(m\) \(z\)-scores. With \(m\) large (thousands), we can estimate \(f(z)\) nonparametrically.
. . .
Efron’s approach:

Estimate \(f(z)\) from a histogram or density estimate of the observed \(z\)-scores
Assume \(f_0\) is known (or estimate it—the “empirical null”)
Estimate \(\pi_0\) from the central peak of the histogram

. . .
This is empirical Bayes: we use the aggregate data to estimate the prior, then apply Bayes’ rule case by case.

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
The Local False Discovery Rate
\[\text{fdr}(z) = P(\text{null} \mid Z = z) = \frac{\pi_0 f_0(z)}{f(z)}\]

This is a posterior probability—the chance that a specific case with \(z\)-score \(z\) is a false discovery.
Contrast with the (tail-area) FDR, which is an expected proportion over a set of rejections.


Relationship
\[\text{FDR}(z_0) = E[\text{fdr}(Z) \mid Z \geq z_0] = \frac{\int_{z_0}^\infty \text{fdr}(z) f(z) \, dz}{\int_{z_0}^\infty f(z) \, dz}\]
The tail-area FDR is the average of the local FDR over the rejection region.

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
Connections to Bayesian Thinking
The two-groups model is inherently Bayesian:

Prior: \(P(\text{null}) = \pi_0\)
Likelihood: \(f_0(z)\) under the null, \(f_1(z)\) under the alternative
Posterior: \(\text{fdr}(z) = P(\text{null} \mid z)\)


But it’s empirical Bayes: instead of specifying a prior, we estimate \(\pi_0\) and \(f_1\) from the data.

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
Efron’s Prostate Cancer Surprise
Efron analyzed a prostate cancer microarray study (Singh et al., 2002): 6,033 genes, 102 patients.
He converted the test statistics to \(z\)-scores expecting them to follow \(N(0,1)\) under the null. Instead, the central peak of the histogram was wider than expected: the empirical null was closer to \(N(\hat{\delta}_0, \hat{\sigma}_0^2)\) with \(\hat{\sigma}_0 > 1\).

Using the theoretical \(N(0,1)\) null would overstate significance — genes whose \(z\)-scores fell in the inflated tails would be called discoveries when they were actually consistent with the (wider) true null.


The “empirical null” saved the analysis — and showed that blindly trusting \(N(0,1)\) can be dangerous. The exact parameters depend on the dataset and estimation method; the key insight is that \(\hat{\sigma}_0 \neq 1\).

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
The Empirical Null
Why the theoretical null might be wrong
Efron observed that in many real datasets, the central peak of the \(z\)-score histogram is wider than \(N(0,1)\).
Possible causes:

Unobserved covariates (batch effects, population structure)
Correlation among tests
Model misspecification

. . .
Empirical null
Fit \(f_0(z) = N(\hat{\delta}_0, \hat{\sigma}_0^2)\) from the central part of the observed \(z\)-score distribution.
If \(\hat{\sigma}_0 > 1\), the theoretical null is too narrow and we’d get too many false discoveries.

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
Empirical Null: Illustration


Code
from scipy.stats import norm

rng = np.random.default_rng(123)
m, m1 = 5000, 200
z_null = rng.normal(loc=0.2, scale=1.3, size=m - m1)  # empirical null ≠ N(0,1)
z_alt = rng.normal(loc=3, scale=1, size=m1)
z = np.concatenate([z_null, z_alt])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.5))
ax.hist(z, bins=80, density=True, color="lightblue", edgecolor="white")
x_grid = np.linspace(z.min(), z.max(), 300)
ax.plot(x_grid, norm.pdf(x_grid, 0, 1), color="red", linewidth=2, linestyle="--",
        label="Theoretical N(0,1)")
ax.plot(x_grid, norm.pdf(x_grid, 0.2, 1.3), color="blue", linewidth=2,
        label="Empirical null N(0.2, 1.3²)")
ax.set_title("Observed z-scores with empirical null")
ax.set_xlabel("z-score")
ax.legend(loc="upper right")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
Efron’s Estimation Procedure
Step by step (Efron, Large-Scale Inference, Ch. 4–5)

Convert test statistics to \(z\)-scores: \(z_i = \Phi^{-1}(1 - p_i)\) or use the statistic directly
Estimate \(f(z)\) using Poisson regression on binned counts (Lindsey’s method)
Estimate \(\pi_0\) and \(f_0\) (theoretical or empirical null)
Compute \(\widehat{\text{fdr}}(z) = \hat{\pi}_0 \hat{f}_0(z) / \hat{f}(z)\)

. . .
The R package locfdr automates this.

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
When Does This Work?
Requirements for empirical Bayes FDR

Large \(m\): Need thousands of tests to estimate \(f(z)\) well
Exchangeability: Tests should be “comparable” (same type of measurement, similar noise structure)
Mixture model is reasonable: The two-groups framework is a good approximation

. . .
When it can fail

Small \(m\) (say \(< 100\)): not enough data to estimate the mixture
Strong correlation structure that distorts the null distribution
Heterogeneous tests (mixing gene expression with methylation, for example)

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes

    C -->|Discovery| E{How many tests?}

title: Lecture 2: False Discovery Rates & Empirical Bayes
text:
Summary

BH procedure: Simple, controls FDR under independence (or PRDS). Your go-to tool.
Storey’s \(q\)-value: Adaptive FDR control using \(\hat{\pi}_0\). More powerful when many nulls.
Efron’s two-groups model: The foundational framework. The mixture \(f = \pi_0 f_0 + (1-\pi_0)f_1\) unifies everything.
Local FDR: A test-specific posterior probability of being null. The most informative single summary.
Empirical null: Always check whether \(f_0 = N(0,1)\) is reasonable. If not, estimate it.

Up Next
Lab 2: Apply BH, \(q\)-values, and local FDR to a real gene-expression dataset.

title: High-Dimensional Data Analysis Workshop
text:
This two-day workshop introduces graduate students in the Biotechnology Training Program to the statistical challenges and modern methods for analyzing high-dimensional data—the setting where the number of measured variables \(p\) far exceeds the number of observations \(n\). This regime is now routine in genomics, proteomics, imaging, and many other areas of biotechnology.



Efron, B. (2010). Large-Scale Inference: Empirical Bayes Methods for Estimation, Testing, and Prediction. Cambridge University Press.
Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R., & Friedman, J. (2009). The Elements of Statistical Learning (2nd ed.). Springer.
Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R., & Wainwright, M. (2015). Statistical Learning with Sparsity. CRC Press.
James, G., Witten, D., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. (2021). An Introduction to Statistical Learning (2nd ed.). Springer.

title: High-Dimensional Data Analysis Workshop
text:
Grading Philosophy
This is a training workshop, not a graded course. Assessments are formative—they exist to consolidate understanding and give you feedback. Engage seriously with them; the value is in the doing.

AI Use
You may use generative AI tools in the labs and homework. Some exercises will ask you to do so explicitly. The assessment focuses on whether you understand what the analysis does, whether it’s correct, and what the results mean.

Purpose
This pre-homework ensures everyone starts Day 1 with a shared foundation in hypothesis testing and \(p\)-values. It also introduces the multiple testing problem and includes a short exercise using AI tools.
Learning Outcomes: LO1, LO3

title: Pre-Homework: Review & Software Check
section: Part 1: Review of Hypothesis Testing (30 min)
text:
Part 1: Review of Hypothesis Testing (30 min)

Reading
Review the basics of hypothesis testing from your introductory statistics course or one of these free references:

OpenIntro Statistics, Ch. 5: openintro.org
An Introduction to Statistical Learning, Ch. 13 (Multiple Testing): statlearning.com



Questions
Q1.1: In your own words, define: (a) Null hypothesis (\(H_0\)), (b) Alternative hypothesis (\(H_1\)), (c) \(p\)-value, (d) Type I error, (e) Type II error.
Q1.2: A researcher tests whether a new drug reduces blood pressure. They report \(p = 0.03\).

State the null and alternative hypotheses.
At significance level \(\alpha = 0.05\), do they reject \(H_0\)?
The \(p\)-value is NOT the probability that \(H_0\) is true. Explain why not, in one or two sentences.

Q1.3: If you perform a test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) and the null hypothesis is actually true, what is the probability of a Type I error? If you perform 20 such independent tests (all with true nulls), what is the probability of getting at least one Type I error?
Hint: \(P(\text{at least one}) = 1 - P(\text{none}) = 1 - (1 - 0.05)^{20}\).

title: Pre-Homework: Review & Software Check
section: Part 2: The Multiple Testing Problem (20 min)
text:
Part 2: The Multiple Testing Problem (20 min)

A thought experiment
Imagine you are a genomics researcher. You have gene expression data for 10,000 genes measured on 40 patients (20 with disease, 20 healthy controls). You run a \(t\)-test for each gene to see if its expression differs between the two groups.
Q2.1: If none of the 10,000 genes are truly associated with the disease, how many genes would you expect to have \(p < 0.05\) by chance alone?
Q2.2: Now suppose 100 of the 10,000 genes are truly associated. The remaining 9,900 null genes still produce approximately \(9{,}900 \times 0.05 \approx 495\) false positives, plus some additional true positives. If you report all genes with \(p < 0.05\) as “significant,” roughly what fraction of your reported genes would be false positives?
Q2.3: Suggest at least one approach to deal with this problem. You don’t need to know the formal name—just describe the idea intuitively.

title: Pre-Homework: Review & Software Check

Exercise: Test the AI on something you know

“What is a \(p\)-value? Explain it in two sentences.”

Record the AI’s response. Then evaluate:

Is the response correct?
Is it complete?
Does it avoid the common mistake of saying “\(p\)-value = probability that \(H_0\) is true”?

AI's response:

Your evaluation:
Q3.2: Now ask the AI a question where it’s more likely to make a mistake:

“I ran 10,000 \(t\)-tests on gene expression data and found 600 genes with \(p < 0.05\). I corrected with Bonferroni and now only 15 genes are significant. A reviewer says I should use FDR instead of Bonferroni. Write 2 sentences explaining why the reviewer might be right.”

Evaluate:

Did the AI correctly explain the difference between FWER and FDR?
Did it explain why FDR is more appropriate for discovery?
Did it get anything wrong or leave anything important out?

AI's response:

Your evaluation:
Q3.3: Ask the AI to write code:

“In R, generate 1000 \(p\)-values from a uniform distribution and make a histogram. Then generate 1000 \(p\)-values where 950 are uniform and 50 are from Beta(0.1, 1). Make a histogram of each. Use base R graphics.”

Run the code. Did it work? If not, what was wrong?

If not, what did you fix?

title: Homework: Day 1 → Day 2

Purpose
This homework consolidates Day 1 material (multiple testing, FDR, critical evaluation). It connects directly to the learning outcomes. You are welcome to use AI for coding tasks, but the conceptual and interpretation questions must reflect your own understanding.

title: Homework: Day 1 → Day 2
text:
Q1: Multiple Testing Concepts (LO1)
A proteomics experiment measures the abundance of \(p = 5{,}000\) proteins in \(n = 30\) tissue samples (15 tumor, 15 normal). For each protein, a two-sample \(t\)-test is performed.
(a) Under the global null (no protein is differentially abundant), how many proteins would you expect to reject at \(\alpha = 0.05\) without any correction?
(b) The Bonferroni-corrected threshold is \(\alpha / m\). Compute this threshold. A protein with a “real” effect has a \(p\)-value of \(3 \times 10^{-4}\). Would it survive Bonferroni correction?
(c) You apply the BH procedure at FDR \(= 0.10\) and get 120 rejections. Give an interpretation of this result in plain language that a biologist would understand.
(d) Your collaborator says “We found 120 significant proteins.” A reviewer replies “But 12 of those are probably false positives!” Is the reviewer being unreasonable? Explain.

title: Homework: Day 1 → Day 2
text:
Q2: FDR in Practice (LO1, LO3)
Using the Golub dataset from Lab 2 (or your own dataset), perform the following analysis. You should use AI to write the code, but you must evaluate its correctness and interpret the results yourself.
(a) Direct an AI to compute BH-adjusted \(p\)-values for the ALL vs. AML comparison using limma (not raw \(t\)-tests). Report:

Number of genes significant at FDR \(< 0.01\), \(< 0.05\), \(< 0.10\)

Include the prompt you used and note whether the AI spontaneously chose limma or whether you had to direct it.
(b) Use the qvalue package to estimate \(\hat{\pi}_0\). What does this estimate tell you about the dataset?
(c) Make a \(p\)-value histogram. Annotate it: mark the region where you think the true alternatives are concentrated, and the region dominated by true nulls.
Include your code (noting what was AI-generated vs. your own) and a one-paragraph interpretation.

title: Homework: Day 1 → Day 2
text:
Q3: Evaluating Analysis Output (LO3)
(a) Write two specifications for the same analysis task: “Perform differential expression analysis on a microarray dataset comparing treatment and control groups.”

Specification A: Vague enough that it would likely produce an incorrect or incomplete analysis.
Specification B: Precise enough that it would likely produce a correct analysis on the first attempt.

Explain what makes Specification B better.
(b) You read (in a paper or AI output) the claim: “Gene XYZ promotes cell proliferation in breast cancer through the PI3K/AKT pathway (Smith et al., 2019).” Describe the steps you would take to verify this before including it in your own work.
(c) Describe one specific error you encountered during today’s labs—whether in AI-generated code, your own code, or a collaborator’s analysis. What was the error? How did you catch it? What statistical knowledge was required to recognize it?
(d) You receive a complete differential expression analysis from a collaborator. It uses raw \(t\)-tests with BH correction on a microarray dataset with 72 samples. Write a short review (3–4 sentences) describing what’s adequate and what should be changed, and why. (Hint: consult the Decision Guide for recommended methods.)

title: Homework: Day 1 → Day 2
text:
Submission
Bring your written answers and code output (for Q2) to Day 2. Be prepared to reference your answers during Lecture 3 and Lecture 4 discussions.

Assessment Criteria
This is a formative assessment. Completeness and engagement matter more than getting every detail right. We are particularly interested in your interpretation of statistical results in your own words and your ability to identify errors in analysis code or output.

title: Decision Guide for High-Dimensional Analysis
text:
1. Multiple Testing: Which Error Rate?





flowchart TD
    A[How many tests?] -->|1–20| B["FWER: Bonferroni or Holm"]
    A -->|"100+"| C{What is the goal?}
    C -->|Confirm a specific hypothesis| D["FWER: Bonferroni or Holm"]
    C -->|Discover candidates for follow-up| E{How many tests?}
    E -->|100–1000| F["BH or Storey q-values"]
    E -->|"1000+"| G{Do you need per-test probabilities?}
    G -->|No — just a list| F
    G -->|Yes| H["Efron local FDR"]















Situation
Method
Controls
R function




Few tests, confirmatory
Bonferroni
FWER \(\leq \alpha\)
p.adjust(method="bonferroni")


Few tests, want more power
Holm
FWER \(\leq \alpha\)
p.adjust(method="holm")


Many tests, discovery
Benjamini–Hochberg
FDR \(\leq \alpha\)
p.adjust(method="BH")


Many tests, want \(\hat{\pi}_0\)
Storey \(q\)-value
FDR \(\leq \alpha\) (adaptive)
qvalue::qvalue()


Thousands of tests, want per-test probability
Local FDR
\(P(\text{null} \mid z)\)
locfdr::locfdr()



Always check: Does your \(p\)-value histogram look right? If the central peak of the \(z\)-score distribution is wider than \(N(0,1)\), the theoretical null is wrong—use the empirical null.

title: Decision Guide for High-Dimensional Analysis
text:
2. Differential Expression: Which Test?





flowchart TD
    A[Data type?] -->|Microarray, log-transformed| B["limma: lmFit → eBayes"]
    A -->|RNA-seq counts| C["DESeq2 or edgeR"]
    A -->|Other continuous| D{Sample size per group?}
    D -->|"Large (n > 30/group)"| E["t-tests + BH correction"]
    D -->|"Small (n < 30/group)"| F["limma (borrows strength across features)"]






Why not raw \(t\)-tests? With small samples, variance estimates are noisy. limma uses empirical Bayes to shrink gene-specific variance estimates toward a common value, producing more stable (moderated) \(t\)-statistics and better power.

title: Decision Guide for High-Dimensional Analysis
text:
3. Dimension Reduction: Which Method?





flowchart TD
    A[Goal?] -->|Preprocessing for downstream analysis| B["PCA"]
    A -->|Visualization| C{Need interpretable axes?}
    A -->|Denoising| B
    C -->|Yes| B
    C -->|No — want cluster separation| D{Dataset size?}
    D -->|"n < 5000"| E["t-SNE (on top PCs)"]
    D -->|"n > 5000"| F["UMAP (on top PCs)"]







How many PCs to keep?







Method
When to use




Scree plot (look for elbow)
Quick visual check; always do this first


Cumulative variance \(\geq 80\%\)
Simple rule of thumb


Marchenko–Pastur threshold
Formal: keep eigenvalues above \((1 + \sqrt{p/n})^2\)


Parallel analysis
Compare to eigenvalues from permuted data





What NOT to conclude from t-SNE / UMAP

Distances between clusters are meaningful → No. Distorted.
Cluster sizes reflect true population sizes → No. Distorted.
The number of visible clusters = number of real groups → Not necessarily. Artifacts can split or merge groups.
A trajectory from A to B is real → Needs independent evidence.

Always: Run PCA first. Apply t-SNE/UMAP to the top \(k\) PCs, not to the raw data.

title: Decision Guide for High-Dimensional Analysis
text:
4. Penalized Regression: Which Penalty?





flowchart TD
    A["Goal?"] -->|Prediction only| B{Expected signal structure?}
    A -->|Variable selection + prediction| C{Correlated predictors?}
    B -->|Many small effects| D["Ridge (α = 0)"]
    B -->|Few large effects| E["LASSO (α = 1)"]
    B -->|Unsure| F["Elastic net (α = 0.5), tune α by CV"]
    C -->|No| E
    C -->|"Yes (e.g., co-regulated genes)"| G["Elastic net (0 < α < 1)"]















Method
Penalty
Sparsity?
Use when




Ridge
\(\lambda\|\beta\|_2^2\)
No
Many small effects, want stable prediction


LASSO
\(\lambda\|\beta\|_1\)
Yes
Few large effects, want interpretable model


Elastic net
\(\lambda[\alpha\|\beta\|_1 + \frac{1-\alpha}{2}\|\beta\|_2^2]\)
Yes
Correlated predictors



Penalty parameterization follows glmnet; some textbooks use different scaling conventions.

Choosing \(\lambda\)

\(\lambda_{\min}\): Minimizes CV error. Best prediction. More variables selected.
\(\lambda_{1\text{se}}\): Largest \(\lambda\) within 1 SE of the minimum. Sparser, more interpretable.
For a gene signature: report \(\lambda_{1\text{se}}\).
For prediction accuracy: report \(\lambda_{\min}\).



Stability
LASSO selection can be unstable—run bootstrap resampling and report genes selected in \(>50\%\) of resamples.

title: Decision Guide for High-Dimensional Analysis
section: 5. Putting It Together: A Typical Workflow
text:
5. Putting It Together: A Typical Workflow





flowchart TD
    A[High-dimensional dataset] --> B[Explore: PCA, check for batch effects]
    B --> C{Analysis goal?}
    C -->|Which features differ between groups?| D[Differential expression: limma/DESeq2 + FDR]
    C -->|Visualize sample structure| E[PCA → t-SNE or UMAP]
    C -->|Predict outcome from features| F[Penalized regression: LASSO/elastic net + CV]
    C -->|All of the above| G[Do all three and compare gene lists]
    D --> H[Volcano plot, gene set enrichment]
    E --> I[Color by known covariates, check for batch effects]
    F --> J[Bootstrap stability, interpret selected features]
    G --> K[Overlap: FDR genes ∩ LASSO genes?]







Key questions to ask at each stage







Stage
Question




Before anything
Is the data normalized? Are there batch effects?


Multiple testing
Is the \(p\)-value histogram flat in the right half? Is the empirical null close to \(N(0,1)\)?


Dimension reduction
Did I scale the data? Did I run t-SNE/UMAP on PCs (not raw data)?


Penalized regression
Did I use family = "binomial" for classification? Did I check stability?


Biological interpretation
Did I verify gene functions against a primary database (not just AI output)?

title: Decision Guide for High-Dimensional Analysis
text:
6. Verifying Biological Claims
Whether the claim comes from a paper, a collaborator, or an AI:

Look up genes on GeneCards or NCBI Gene
Check cited papers on PubMed—do they exist? Do they say what’s claimed?
Cross-reference with pathway databases: KEGG, Gene Ontology
For gene set enrichment: use clusterProfiler (R) or Enrichr (web)

title: Workshop Design
text:
Each lecture is anchored by a real scientific story — a finding that surprised, failed, or changed a field. Students remember the story, learn the statistics through it, and connect the method to the problem it solves. The story is the mental handle; the method is what the story teaches. Some stories involve spectacular failure (5-HTTLPR, the dead salmon, the Duke fraud), some involve unexpected success (MammaPrint, Allen Brain Atlas), and some involve beautiful mistakes (Cavalli-Sforza’s maps). Each is memorable, concrete, and true.











Lecture
Named Story
Method It Teaches




1: “The Gene That Wasn’t There”
Caspi 2003 / Border 2019 (5-HTTLPR)
Multiple testing, FWER, p-value histogram


1
Dead salmon fMRI (Bennett et al.)
Cost of not correcting


2: “The Histogram That Was Too Wide”
Efron’s prostate cancer z-scores
Empirical null, two-groups model, local FDR


2
Efron & Morris baseball shrinkage
Empirical Bayes, James-Stein estimation


2
Open Science Collaboration (2015)
Replication crisis as calibration failure


3: “The Map That Drew Itself”
Cavalli-Sforza’s PCA maps / Novembre 2008
PCA artifacts on spatial data


3
Netflix Prize (2006)
Low-rank approximation, latent factors


3
Allen Brain Cell Atlas (single-cell)
UMAP as genuine discovery tool


4: “Seventy Genes and a Fraud”
van ’t Veer 2002 / MINDACT trial
Penalized regression, external validation


4
Anil Potti / Duke fraud
Reproducibility, cross-validation leakage


4
Hoerl & Kennard 1970
Ridge regression, bias-variance tradeoff


4
Tibshirani 1996
LASSO, geometry of sparsity






Each 45–60 minute lecture is an essay structured as three to six connected sections (~8–12 minutes each). Each section has:

A named story or vivid example: teach through narrative, make it concrete.
A statistical idea: the formal concept the story illustrates — definition, method, diagnostic.
A “but” turn: the limitation, failure mode, or complication that motivates the next section.

The sections are loosely coupled: students who lose the thread in one section can re-enter at the next story. The “but” turns create forward momentum — each answer raises a new question.




“What does the histogram tell you?” — p-value histogram (L1–L2), z-score distribution (L2), scree plot (L3), coefficient path (L4).
“Would this replicate?” — 5-HTTLPR didn’t (L1). 64% of psychology didn’t (L2). PCA artifacts replicate perfectly but mean nothing (L3). LASSO picks different genes on different bootstrap samples (L4).
“What are you actually asking?” — FWER vs. FDR (L1–L2). Visualization vs. classification (L3). Prediction vs. inference (L4).




Each lecture is immediately followed by a hands-on lab. The lecture provides conceptual understanding through narrative; the lab provides procedural understanding through code. Students hear the story, then build the analysis themselves on the same dataset.








Lecture
Paired Lab
Students Will…




L1: The Gene That Wasn’t There
Lab 1: The Multiple Testing Problem
Run 3,051 t-tests, read the p-value histogram, apply Bonferroni vs. BH


L2: The Histogram That Was Too Wide
Lab 2: FDR in Practice
Apply BH and Storey’s q-values to the Golub dataset, estimate \(\pi_0\)


L3: The Map That Drew Itself
Lab 3: Dimension Reduction
Run PCA and UMAP on the Golub dataset, read scree plots, compare projections


L4: Seventy Genes and a Fraud
Lab 4: Penalized Regression
Fit ridge/LASSO/elastic net, examine coefficient paths, cross-validate

title: Workshop Design
text:
Enduring Understandings
These are the big ideas students should internalize and carry beyond the workshop.

Classical statistical inference breaks down when \(p \gg n\), and the breakdown is predictable. Testing thousands of hypotheses at the standard 5% level guarantees hundreds of false positives. This is not a flaw in the statistics — it is the statistics working exactly as designed for a single test, applied to a problem it was not designed for. The tools to handle this exist; the challenge is knowing when and how to use them.
High-dimensional data always shows structure — the question is whether the structure is real. PCA produces gradients. UMAP produces clusters. LASSO produces gene lists. These methods are designed to find patterns, and they will find patterns even in pure noise. The discipline of high-dimensional analysis is validation: checking whether the patterns replicate, whether they correspond to known biology, and whether they survive alternative analysis choices.
Borrowing information across features transforms the multiple testing problem from a curse into a resource. When you test thousands of genes, you have thousands of problems — but you also have thousands of data points about the behavior of the null. Empirical Bayes methods (Efron’s two-groups model, Storey’s q-values, limma’s moderated t-statistics) exploit this: the ensemble teaches you about each individual test. This is the central insight of large-scale inference.
The gap between fitting data and predicting outcomes is the core challenge of high-dimensional modeling. With \(p > n\), any method can achieve perfect fit on training data. The bias-variance tradeoff — deliberately introducing bias through regularization to reduce variance — is not a compromise but a necessity. The choice of regularizer (L1, L2, elastic net) encodes assumptions about the structure of the signal.
Reproducibility is a statistical property, not just an ethical norm. The difference between MammaPrint (saved lives) and the Duke fraud (harmed patients) is not statistical sophistication — both used penalized regression on microarray data. The difference is validation discipline: proper cross-validation, external replication, and honesty about uncertainty. Reproducibility requires both statistical tools and the judgment to use them correctly.

title: Workshop Design
text:
Essential Questions
These are recurring, open-ended questions that thread across all four lectures and labs, provoking deeper inquiry.

When should you believe a statistical finding, and what would it take to change your mind? (Threads through: 5-HTTLPR survival for 15 years (L1), the replication crisis (L2), PCA artifacts that look convincing (L3), MammaPrint vs. Duke fraud (L4))
What does the histogram tell you — and what can’t it tell you? (Threads through: p-value histogram as diagnostic (L1), z-score distribution and empirical null (L2), scree plot (L3), coefficient path (L4))
When you run thousands of tests simultaneously, who benefits — and what are the hidden costs? (Threads through: multiple testing problem (L1), FDR as a negotiated tradeoff (L2), overclustering in UMAP (L3), LASSO instability (L4))
How do you tell the difference between a pattern in the data and a pattern in the method? (Threads through: Bonferroni as conservative overcorrection (L1), empirical vs. theoretical null (L2), Cavalli-Sforza’s PCA maps (L3), overfitting in p >> n (L4))
What does it mean to use a computational tool responsibly — and what goes wrong when you don’t? (Threads through: dead salmon (L1), winner’s curse and publication bias (L2), UMAP hallucinations (L3), Duke fraud (L4), AI-generated analysis in all labs)

title: Workshop Design
text:
Transfer Goals
By the end of this workshop, students should be able to:

Given a high-dimensional dataset, diagnose the testing regime (how many tests, what error rate to control) and select an appropriate correction procedure (Bonferroni, BH, q-values) with justification.
Read a p-value histogram and identify signal, noise, the null proportion \(\pi_0\), and signs of problems (anti-conservative or conservative behavior).
Apply PCA and UMAP to a biological dataset, interpret the output critically (scree plots, loadings, cluster stability), and articulate what the projection does and does not show.
Fit regularized regression models (ridge, LASSO, elastic net) using cross-validation, interpret coefficient paths, and explain why variable selection is unstable.
Evaluate computational output — from their own code or from AI — by checking for common errors, questioning default choices, and verifying interpretations against primary sources.
Articulate the difference between exploratory visualization, confirmatory testing, and predictive modeling, and choose methods appropriate to each goal.

title: Workshop Design
text:
Assessment Philosophy
This is a two-day training workshop, not a graded course. All assessments are formative — they exist to consolidate understanding and provide feedback, not to assign grades. The value is in the doing.
Assessments serve three roles:

Preparation (Pre-Homework): Activate prerequisite knowledge and verify software setup before Day 1. Students arrive ready to engage.
Practice (Labs 1–4): Build procedural skills on the same dataset used in the lectures. Labs pair with lectures: hear the story, then build the analysis.
Integration (Homework): Connect ideas across lectures, apply methods to new scenarios, and practice evaluating AI-generated analysis.


AI Use
AI tools are permitted and explicitly used in every lab and in the homework. Some exercises require students to prompt AI, evaluate its output, and catch errors. Assessment focuses on whether students understand what the analysis does, whether it’s correct, and what the results mean — not on whether they can code everything from scratch.

title: Workshop Design
text:
Lab Design Principles

Each lab follows the structure: setup \(\to\) write code \(\to\) use AI \(\to\) critique checklist.
Labs use the Golub dataset (3,051 genes \(\times\) 72 samples) throughout — continuity across all four labs builds familiarity and allows comparison across methods.
Labs include deliberately subtle errors for students to identify — training critical evaluation, not just execution.
AI extension exercises provide a suggested prompt and a critique checklist so students practice evaluating AI output systematically.
Python Colab notebooks require zero local installation — students run everything in a browser.
Reproducibility: numpy.random.seed(2026) and plt.style.use('seaborn-v0_8-whitegrid') in every notebook.

title: Workshop Design
text:
Homework Design Principles

The homework bridges Day 1 and Day 2 — it reinforces large-scale inference concepts (L1–L2) and previews the critical evaluation skills needed for Day 2.
Three questions span different Bloom’s levels:

Q1 (Apply/Analyze): Multiple testing concepts applied to a new scenario (proteomics, 5,000 proteins).
Q2 (Apply/Evaluate): AI-assisted FDR analysis using limma (not raw t-tests) — students evaluate whether AI makes good methodological choices.
Q3 (Evaluate/Create): Evaluate analysis output, write specifications, verify claims, review a collaborator’s analysis.

The homework is not graded. Completeness and engagement matter more than correctness.

title: Workshop Design
text:
Pre-Homework Design Principles

Three parts activate different prerequisites:

Part 1 (Remember): Hypothesis testing vocabulary (H\(_0\), H\(_1\), p-value, Type I/II error).
Part 2 (Analyze): Thought experiment about 10,000 genes — students derive the multiple testing problem intuitively before Lecture 1 formalizes it.
Part 3 (Evaluate): First encounter with AI evaluation — students prompt AI for statistical definitions and assess correctness.

title: Workshop Design
text:
Scaffolding Logic
The workshop follows a two-day arc with a consistent pedagogical rhythm:

Pre-Homework activates prerequisites — students arrive having reviewed hypothesis testing and attempted an intuitive version of the multiple testing problem.
Lecture introduces concepts through stories — each lecture is an essay with named stories, statistical ideas, and “but” turns that motivate the next idea.
Lab practices the skills on the same dataset — students build the analysis they just heard about, on the Golub dataset, with AI assistance.
Homework integrates and transfers — students apply Day 1 concepts to new scenarios and practice evaluating AI output.
Day 2 builds on Day 1 — dimension reduction (L3) and penalized regression (L4) use the same dataset and the same critical evaluation framework, but for different statistical problems.

The connecting thread is the Golub dataset: 3,051 genes, 72 patients, ALL vs. AML leukemia. Every lecture, every lab, and the homework use this dataset. By the end, students have seen the same data through four different statistical lenses.

title: Workshop Design
text:
Lecture Plan
Four lectures across two days. Each lecture is a multi-section essay (~45–60 minutes) followed by a one-hour hands-on lab.

Day 1: Large-Scale Inference
Primary textbook: Efron (2010), Large-Scale Inference, Ch. 1–7
Lecture 1 — “The Gene That Wasn’t There”
How false positives survive — and how a histogram catches them.








Section
Named Story
Statistical Idea




1.1
Caspi 2003: 5-HTTLPR and depression (8,000 citations, then nothing)
Hypothesis testing logic; one test works, many tests break


1.2
Golub dataset: 3,051 genes, 72 patients
Multiple testing from first principles; 3,051 \(\times\) 0.05 \(\approx\) 153 expected false positives


1.3
Two histograms: pure-null (uniform) vs. Golub (spike + flat)
P-value histogram as diagnostic; excess near zero = signal; flat height \(\approx \pi_0\)


1.4
Bonferroni: \(0.05/3{,}051 \approx 1.6 \times 10^{-5}\); GWAS threshold: \(5 \times 10^{-8}\)
Bonferroni and FWER; controls the wrong thing for discovery


1.5
Dead salmon with “brain activity” (Bennett et al.)
Voxelwise testing without correction; 25–40% of fMRI studies didn’t correct


1.6
Border 2019: 18 candidate genes, 62K–443K people, none replicate
Publication bias, winner’s curse, garden of forking paths




Lab 1 follows immediately: students run the 3,051 tests themselves.

Lecture 2 — “The Histogram That Was Too Wide”
From Benjamini–Hochberg to Efron’s local FDR.








Section
Named Story
Statistical Idea




2.1
Benjamini & Hochberg 1995 (2-page paper, now 60K+ citations)
FDR definition: \(E[V/R]\); the BH procedure; controls FDR under independence


2.2
Efron’s prostate cancer z-scores: the center was too wide
Theoretical vs. empirical null; sources of null inflation (correlations, batch effects)


2.3
The mixture model: \(f(z) = \pi_0 f_0(z) + (1-\pi_0) f_1(z)\)
Two-groups model; local fdr: \(\text{fdr}(z) = \pi_0 f_0(z) / f(z)\)


2.4
Efron & Morris baseball shrinkage (1977)
Empirical Bayes; James-Stein; Storey’s q-values; borrowing strength


2.5
Open Science Collaboration 2015: 97% \(\to\) 36% replication
Replication crisis as predicted by FDR framework when \(\pi_0\) is high and power is low




Lab 2 follows after lunch: students apply BH and q-values to the Golub dataset.




Day 2: Structure Discovery and Modern Tools
Primary textbook: Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman (2009), Elements of Statistical Learning, Ch. 3, 14, 18
Lecture 3 — “The Map That Drew Itself”
Finding structure in high-dimensional data — and learning not to trust everything you see.








Section
Named Story
Statistical Idea




3.1
Cavalli-Sforza 1978: PCA maps of European gene frequencies
PCA finds orthogonal directions of max variance; eigenvectors on spatial grids produce sinusoidal artifacts


3.2
Golub dataset: PC1 vs. PC2 separates ALL from AML
Scree plot, loadings, biplots; Marchenko-Pastur law for distinguishing signal from noise


3.3
Netflix Prize 2006: matrix factorization for recommendations
SVD and low-rank approximation; choosing rank; latent factors


3.4
Allen Brain Cell Atlas: 5,000+ cell types from UMAP
t-SNE and UMAP; neighborhood graphs; what is preserved (local clusters) and what is not (distances)


3.5
UMAP on permuted Golub data: clusters in pure noise
Stability analysis; validation strategies; PCA as GWAS covariate (Price 2006)




Lab 3 follows immediately: students run PCA and UMAP on the Golub dataset.

Lecture 4 — “Seventy Genes and a Fraud”
Regularization, variable selection, and the discipline of validation.








Section
Named Story
Statistical Idea




4.1
98 patients, 25,000 genes: perfect fit, zero information
Rank deficiency when \(p > n\); overfitting guaranteed; bias-variance at its extreme


4.2
Hoerl & Kennard 1970: deliberately biased estimates predict better
Ridge: \(\|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2 + \lambda\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2\); regularization path; cross-validation


4.3
Tibshirani 1996: L1 geometry produces sparsity
LASSO: diamond vs. circle; corners \(\Rightarrow\) exact zeros; instability of selected variables


4.4
Zou & Hastie 2005: correlated genes need grouping
Elastic net: \(\alpha\) mixing parameter; grouping property; stable sparse models


4.5
van ’t Veer 2002 / MINDACT 2016: 46% spared chemotherapy
External validation; why MammaPrint worked (strong signal, proper CV, prospective trial)


4.6
Potti / Duke fraud: 18 retractions, patients harmed
Cross-validation leakage; mislabeled samples; reproducibility as statistical practice

title: Lecture 1: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
In 2003, Avshalom Caspi and a team of researchers published a paper in Science that would become one of the most influential findings in psychiatric genetics. They reported that a variant in the serotonin transporter gene — a gene called 5-HTTLPR — predicted whether people who experienced stressful life events would develop depression. The idea was elegant: some people carry a version of this gene that produces less serotonin transporter protein, and those people, when exposed to stress, are more vulnerable to depression. Nature plus nurture, mediated by a single gene.
The paper was cited over 8,000 times.1 It launched an entire subfield. Hundreds of labs tried to replicate and extend the finding. Graduate students built dissertations around it. Textbooks cited it as a model of gene-environment interaction. If you took a psychology or genetics course between 2005 and 2015, you probably heard about it.
In 2019, a team led by Richard Border tested the 5-HTTLPR hypothesis — along with 17 other “candidate gene” hypotheses for depression — in samples ranging from 62,000 to 443,000 people. They had preregistered their analysis plan. They used the same measures and the same statistical approaches that the original studies had used, just with vastly more data.
They found nothing. Not “a smaller effect than expected.” Nothing. The serotonin transporter variant had no detectable relationship to depression, with or without stress. Neither did any of the other 17 candidate genes.
How does a false positive survive for fifteen years?
The short answer is: it wasn’t one false positive. It was thousands of them, across hundreds of studies, in a field that was running many statistical tests without accounting for how many tests it was running. And the tools to prevent this — to control the rate of false discovery when you’re testing many hypotheses — existed the entire time. The field just wasn’t using them.
That’s what this lecture is about. Not the serotonin gene specifically, but the general problem: what happens when you test many hypotheses, and what it looks like when you don’t account for it. We’re going to see this problem firsthand, using a real dataset that we’ll work with for the rest of the workshop. And we’ll start building the diagnostic tools to catch it.

title: Lecture 1: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:

title: Lecture 1: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
Three thousand and fifty-one tests
Let’s start with a dataset. In 1999, Todd Golub and colleagues published a landmark paper in Science that asked a deceptively simple question: can you distinguish two types of leukemia — acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) and acute myeloid leukemia (AML) — using only gene expression? They profiled 72 patients on Affymetrix microarrays, measuring thousands of genes simultaneously. After preprocessing, the dataset contains 3,051 genes per patient. We’ll call it the Golub dataset, and we’ll work with it for the rest of the workshop.
The 72 patients fall into two groups: 47 with ALL and 25 with AML. These are different diseases — they have different prognoses, different treatment responses, different biology. The question is: which genes are expressed differently between the two types?
This is a perfectly reasonable question. A clinician might want to know which genes distinguish the types so they can develop better diagnostics. A biologist might want to understand the molecular basis of the distinction. An informaticist might want to build a classifier.
So you do what anyone would do. You take each gene, one at a time, and ask: is this gene expressed at a different level in ALL patients than in AML patients? The standard tool is a t-test. You compute a test statistic, you get a p-value, and you decide whether the difference is “statistically significant.”
Do this for all 3,051 genes and you get 3,051 p-values.
Now, here’s the thing about p-values that everyone learns in their first statistics course and then immediately forgets the implications of: a p-value of 0.05 means that if the gene is not differentially expressed — if there is truly no difference between ALL and AML — there’s still a 5% chance of getting a test statistic this extreme or more extreme, just by chance. That’s fine for one test. You accept a 5% false positive rate as the cost of doing business.
But you’re not running one test. You’re running 3,051.
If none of the genes were actually different between ALL and AML — if the two diseases were molecularly identical, which they’re not, but bear with me — you’d still expect about 5% of your tests to come back significant at the 0.05 level. Five percent of 3,051 is about 153. You’d get 153 “significant” results from pure noise.
The false positive rate is still 5%. The false positive count is 153. And if you’re a biologist looking at your results, you don’t see a rate — you see a list of genes. A long list. A list that looks like you’ve discovered something.
This is the multiple testing problem. It’s not subtle. The math is first-semester probability. And yet it fooled an entire field of psychiatric genetics for fifteen years, and it still catches people in every discipline that runs many tests on the same dataset. (And 3,051 genes is modest by modern standards — today’s RNA-seq experiments measure 20,000 or more.)
Let’s look at what it actually looks like.

title: Lecture 1: The Gene That Wasn’t There
section: Your first diagnostic: the p-value histogram
text:
Your first diagnostic: the p-value histogram
I want to show you the single most useful plot in high-dimensional statistics. It’s not a volcano plot. It’s not a heatmap. It’s a histogram of p-values.
Take those 3,051 p-values from the Golub dataset and plot them in a histogram. The x-axis goes from 0 to 1. The y-axis counts how many p-values fall in each bin.
Here’s what you’d see if none of the genes were differentially expressed: a flat histogram. Uniform. Equal numbers of p-values in every bin. This is because, under the null hypothesis, p-values are uniformly distributed between 0 and 1. That’s not an approximation or an asymptotic result — it’s exact.2 If nothing is going on, every bin gets the same number of p-values.
Now consider the Golub data. The histogram is not flat. There is a pronounced spike near zero — hundreds of genes with very small p-values — and then a roughly flat region for the rest. The spike is signal: genes that are genuinely differentially expressed between ALL and AML. The flat part is the null genes — the genes where no real difference is present, producing the uniform distribution we would expect.
This one plot tells you three things:
First, whether there’s signal. If the histogram is flat, there is nothing to detect. If there’s a spike near zero, something real is happening.
Second, roughly how much signal. The height of the flat part tells you the density of null p-values. If the flat part is at, say, 80% of the total, then roughly 80% of your genes are null and 20% are non-null. That’s a lot of signal. In the Golub data, the spike is substantial — ALL and AML are genuinely different diseases, and the transcriptomic differences are large.
Third, whether your analysis is trustworthy. A well-behaved analysis produces a histogram with a flat right side and a spike on the left. But sometimes the histogram looks wrong. If the bars increase toward the right — more large p-values than expected — your test is conservative (too cautious). If there’s a bump in the middle, something is off with your test statistics. If the entire histogram is shifted left with no flat region, your null model is wrong. These pathological histograms are surprisingly common, and they tell you to stop and fix your analysis before interpreting any results.
I want you to internalize this: before you look at gene lists, before you compute adjusted p-values, before you run any correction procedure — plot the histogram. It takes ten seconds and it tells you whether the rest of your analysis is worth doing.
We’ll come back to this histogram in Lecture 2, where it turns from a diagnostic into an estimator. The flat part isn’t just telling you “these genes are null” — it’s telling you the proportion of null genes, and that proportion is the key ingredient in false discovery rate estimation. But I’m getting ahead of myself.

title: Lecture 1: The Gene That Wasn’t There
section: Bonferroni and family-wise error rate control
text:
Bonferroni and family-wise error rate control
So you have 3,051 p-values and a histogram that shows real signal. You want to call some genes significant. But you know that if you use the standard 0.05 threshold, you’ll get over a hundred false positives mixed in with the real results. What do you do?
The oldest and simplest answer is Bonferroni correction. The idea is almost embarrassingly straightforward: divide your significance level by the number of tests. Instead of calling a gene significant at p < 0.05, you call it significant at p < 0.05 / 3,051 ≈ 1.6 × 10⁻⁵.
The logic goes like this. You want the probability of making any false positive — even one — to be at most 5%. This is called the family-wise error rate, or FWER. If each individual test has a false positive probability of α/m, then by the union bound, the probability that at least one of the m tests produces a false positive is at most m × (α/m) = α. Done.
Bonferroni is clean, easy to explain, and requires no assumptions about the dependence structure of your tests. It works whether your genes are independent or correlated. It works whether you’re testing 10 hypotheses or 10 million. In genome-wide association studies, where you’re testing around a million genetic variants, the convention is a significance threshold of 5 × 10⁻⁸ — which is just Bonferroni at the million-variant scale.3
And Bonferroni works, in the sense that it does what it promises. If you apply Bonferroni to 3,051 tests, the probability that any of your declared significant genes is a false positive is at most 5%.
But here’s the problem: Bonferroni controls the wrong thing.
Think about what the family-wise error rate actually means. It’s the probability of any false positive. Any. Even one. In a list of 500 significant genes, Bonferroni demands a guarantee that not a single one is a false discovery. That’s an extraordinarily high standard. And to achieve it, the threshold becomes so stringent that you miss most of the real signal.
If you’re an astronomer and you’re about to announce the discovery of gravitational waves, yes, you want to be very sure you haven’t made even one false positive. The standard there is “five sigma,” roughly one in 3.5 million. The cost of a false claim is enormous.
But if you’re a biologist screening thousands of genes to find candidates for follow-up experiments, you’re in a completely different situation. You don’t need a guarantee that every gene on your list is real. You need a useful list — one where most of the genes are real and the false positives are a manageable fraction. If your list of 500 genes contains 25 false positives, that’s a 5% false discovery rate, and for most biological applications, that’s fine. You’ll validate the interesting ones in follow-up experiments anyway.
Bonferroni can’t give you that list. It’s trying to prevent a different kind of error — any error at all — and in doing so, it throws away most of your real discoveries.
You need a different question. Not “did I make any false positives?” but “what fraction of my discoveries are false?” That question has an answer, and it’s the subject of Lecture 2. But before we get there, I want to show you what happens when people skip the correction entirely.

title: Lecture 1: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
The dead salmon
In 2009, a neuroscientist named Craig Bennett and his colleagues put a dead Atlantic salmon in an fMRI scanner.4
They showed the salmon a series of photographs of people in social situations and asked it — well, they ran the standard fMRI analysis pipeline — to determine the emotional valence of each photograph. The scanner measured blood oxygenation levels across thousands of tiny volume elements (voxels) in the salmon’s body. For each voxel, a statistical test asked: does the signal in this voxel change in response to the photographs?
Without any correction for multiple comparisons, a cluster of voxels in the salmon’s brain cavity showed statistically significant “neural activity.” The dead fish appeared to be processing human emotions.
With a standard correction for multiple comparisons, the signal disappeared. Because of course it did — the salmon was dead.
Bennett presented this as a poster at a conference, and it became widely circulated, partly for its memorable framing and partly because it made a serious point: every fMRI analysis involves thousands of voxels, each tested independently, and the multiple testing problem is the same as in genomics. Bennett surveyed published fMRI studies and found that between 25% and 40% did not report correcting for multiple comparisons. The poster was memorable. The survey was concerning.
The salmon is the same problem we saw with genes, just in a different domain. Instead of 3,051 genes, you have tens of thousands of voxels. Instead of expression levels, you have blood oxygenation signals. But the statistics are the same: many tests, a 5% false positive rate per test, and enough tests that false positives are guaranteed.
I’m telling you the salmon story because it’s memorable and because it makes the multiple testing problem feel visceral in a way that abstract calculations don’t. But the salmon was caught quickly — it was a deliberate demonstration. The next story is about what happens when false positives aren’t caught at all.

title: Lecture 1: The Gene That Wasn’t There
section: What a field-wide false positive looks like
text:
What a field-wide false positive looks like
Let’s go back to the serotonin gene.
Caspi et al. published their finding in 2003. Over the next fifteen years, hundreds of studies tried to replicate or extend the result. Some found the same thing. Some found the opposite. Some found nothing. Meta-analyses — studies that combine the results of many studies — disagreed with each other, depending on which studies they included and how they weighted them.
This should have been a red flag. When a real effect exists, replications generally agree on its direction and approximate size. When studies disagree about whether an effect is positive, negative, or zero, that’s what noise looks like.
But the field had reasons to keep believing. The biology was plausible — serotonin is genuinely involved in mood regulation. The original study was well-designed by the standards of its time. And there were enough positive replications to maintain hope, especially if you squinted at the inclusion criteria of the meta-analyses.
In 2019, Border et al. settled it. They tested 18 candidate gene hypotheses for depression — including 5-HTTLPR — in enormous samples, with preregistered analysis plans and consistent methodology across all 18 tests. None of the 18 replicated. Not one.
Meanwhile, a parallel approach had been yielding real results. Genome-wide association studies, which test hundreds of thousands or millions of genetic variants and apply stringent multiple testing correction (that 5 × 10⁻⁸ threshold), had been steadily finding genetic loci associated with depression. By 2019, GWAS had identified over 100 loci. These weren’t the same genes that the candidate gene studies had focused on. The two approaches — one without proper correction, one with — were studying the same biology and getting completely different answers.
How did the false positives survive? Three mechanisms, each reinforcing the others:
Publication bias. Studies that found a significant result for 5-HTTLPR were more likely to be published. Studies that found nothing were more likely to sit in a file drawer. The published literature was a biased sample of the evidence.
Winner’s curse. The studies that did get published — the ones that found significant results — necessarily overestimated the effect size. To cross the significance threshold with a small, noisy sample, the observed effect has to be larger than the true effect. So the early estimates of the 5-HTTLPR effect were inflated, and later studies with more realistic estimates looked like “failures to replicate.”
The garden of forking paths. There are many defensible choices in any analysis: which covariates to include, which subgroups to analyze, which outcome measure to use, how to define “stressful life events.” Each choice is a branch point, and the analysts — without any intent to deceive — could explore different branches until they found one that produced a significant result. This isn’t the same as running thousands of explicit tests, but it has the same effect: the number of implicit tests is much larger than the number of reported tests.
None of this required bad intentions. The researchers who studied 5-HTTLPR were, for the most part, careful scientists who believed in their hypothesis and interpreted ambiguous evidence in its favor. That’s not fraud — that’s human cognition. The problem wasn’t the scientists. The problem was the statistical framework, which didn’t account for the scale of testing that was actually happening.
The fix was known the entire time. GWAS adopted it from the beginning: test everything, correct for everything, accept the stringent thresholds. The candidate gene field tested one or a few genes per study, which seemed like it shouldn’t require correction — but across the entire literature, across hundreds of studies each making dozens of analytical choices, the effective number of tests was enormous. The correction should have happened at the field level, not just the study level.

The 5-HTTLPR story isn’t a story about bad scientists. It’s a story about what happens when a field’s statistical framework doesn’t match the scale of its questions. The framework to handle that scale exists — it’s what we’ll build in Lecture 2. But first, in the lab, you’re going to see it for yourself. You’ll take the Golub dataset, run those 3,051 tests, plot the histogram, apply Bonferroni, and feel the gap between “controls any error” and “gives you a useful answer.” That gap is where the false discovery rate lives.

title: Lecture 1: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
Footnotes


To put that in perspective, a typical well-cited paper in genetics gets maybe 200 citations. A famous one gets 1,000. 8,000 is a field-defining result.↩︎
Technically, this is true for continuous test statistics. Discrete test statistics produce p-values that are stochastically larger than uniform, which makes the test conservative. For gene expression data with continuous measurements, the uniform assumption is essentially exact.↩︎
The actual number 5 × 10⁻⁸ comes from a more sophisticated calculation involving linkage disequilibrium and the effective number of independent tests in the genome, but it’s in the Bonferroni ballpark.↩︎
It was purchased at a local fish market. It was, by all accounts, very dead.↩︎

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
From Faces to Genes
In 1991, Turk and Pentland ran PCA on a database of face photographs. The principal components — ghostly, blurred images they called “eigenfaces” — could reconstruct any face in the database using just 100 numbers instead of thousands of pixels.

This was the moment dimension reduction went from mathematical abstraction to transformative tool: a high-dimensional object (a face) could be faithfully represented in a far lower-dimensional space.


The same idea now drives single-cell genomics, where 30,000 gene measurements per cell are routinely compressed to 30–50 principal components before any analysis.

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
The Problem
We have data \(X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}\) with \(p\) large (thousands to tens of thousands).
Goals:

Visualization: Project to 2D or 3D for exploration
Denoising: Separate signal from noise
Preprocessing: Reduce \(p\) before applying downstream methods (clustering, regression)


Core question: Can we represent the data in a lower-dimensional space \(\mathbb{R}^k\) with \(k \ll p\), preserving the important structure?

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
The idea
Find orthogonal directions of maximum variance in the data.
Formally
Given centered data \(X\) (\(n \times p\)), PCA solves:
\[\mathbf{v}_1 = \arg\max_{\|\mathbf{v}\| = 1} \text{Var}(X\mathbf{v}) = \arg\max_{\|\mathbf{v}\| = 1} \mathbf{v}^T \hat{\Sigma} \mathbf{v}\]
The solution \(\mathbf{v}_1\) is the eigenvector of \(\hat{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1}X^TX\) with the largest eigenvalue.

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
PCA via the SVD
The singular value decomposition of \(X\):
\[X = U D V^T\]

\(U\) (\(n \times r\)): left singular vectors (sample directions)
\(D\) (\(r \times r\)): diagonal matrix of singular values \(d_1 \geq d_2 \geq \cdots \geq 0\)
\(V\) (\(p \times r\)): right singular vectors (loadings = principal component directions)

where \(r = \min(n, p)\). Software returns this “thin” SVD by default.

Connection: Eigenvalues of \(\hat{\Sigma}\) are \(\lambda_j = d_j^2 / (n-1)\).
PC scores: \(Z = XV = UD\) (the \(j\)-th column of \(Z\) is the \(j\)-th principal component).

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
How Many Components?
Scree plot
Plot \(\lambda_j\) (or proportion of variance explained) vs. \(j\). Look for an “elbow.”
Proportion of variance explained
\[\text{PVE}(k) = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^k \lambda_j}{\sum_{j=1}^p \lambda_j}\]
Formal methods

Marchenko–Pastur threshold: Under null (\(\Sigma = I\)) with \(\gamma = p/n\), eigenvalues lie in \([\max(0,\,(1-\sqrt{\gamma})^2),\; (1+\sqrt{\gamma})^2]\). When \(p > n\) (our regime), the lower edge is 0 and only the upper edge matters. Eigenvalues above \((1+\sqrt{\gamma})^2\) are signal.
Parallel analysis: Compare observed eigenvalues to those from permuted data.

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
PCA: Strengths and Limitations
Strengths

Computationally fast; solved by standard linear algebra
Optimal for linear reconstruction (Eckart–Young theorem)
Interpretable loadings: which variables contribute to each PC?

Limitations

Linear only: Cannot capture nonlinear manifold structure
Sensitive to scaling: Must standardize or choose units carefully
Rotation ambiguity: PCs are identified only up to sign
High-D noise bias: In the \(p \gg n\) regime, the Marchenko–Pastur law inflates eigenvalues. Leading PCs can be distorted.

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
Biplots
A biplot overlays sample scores and variable loadings on the same 2D plot.


Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris

iris = load_iris()
X_iris = StandardScaler().fit_transform(iris.data)
pca_iris = PCA(n_components=2).fit(X_iris)
scores = pca_iris.transform(X_iris)
loadings = pca_iris.components_.T  # (4, 2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))
ax.scatter(scores[:, 0], scores[:, 1], s=15, color="gray", alpha=0.6)
scale = np.abs(scores).max() / np.abs(loadings).max() * 0.8
for i, name in enumerate(iris.feature_names):
    ax.arrow(0, 0, loadings[i, 0] * scale, loadings[i, 1] * scale,
             color="red", head_width=0.1, linewidth=1.5)
    ax.text(loadings[i, 0] * scale * 1.15, loadings[i, 1] * scale * 1.15,
            name, color="red", fontsize=9, ha="center")
ax.set_xlabel(f"PC1 ({pca_iris.explained_variance_ratio_[0]:.1%})")
ax.set_ylabel(f"PC2 ({pca_iris.explained_variance_ratio_[1]:.1%})")
ax.set_title("Biplot of Iris Data")
ax.axhline(0, color="gray", linewidth=0.5)
ax.axvline(0, color="gray", linewidth=0.5)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()










Read it as: arrows pointing in the same direction as a cluster of points tell you which variables are high in that cluster.

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
When t-SNE Lies
In 2016, Wattenberg, Viégas, and Johnson published “How to Use t-SNE Effectively” — a landmark interactive article showing that:

t-SNE can create clusters from completely uniform data
It can split a single group into two apparent clusters
It can make distant groups appear adjacent
Cluster sizes and distances between clusters are meaningless


Yet thousands of papers treat t-SNE and UMAP plots as ground truth. A t-SNE plot is a sketch, not a photograph.

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
Beyond PCA: Nonlinear Methods
When the data lies on a nonlinear manifold (common in single-cell biology), PCA may miss important structure.
Three popular nonlinear methods:

t-SNE (van der Maaten & Hinton, 2008)
UMAP (McInnes et al., 2018)
Diffusion maps (Coifman & Lafon, 2006)

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
t-SNE: Intuition
t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding

In high-D: compute pairwise “affinities” \(p_{ij}\) using Gaussian kernels
In low-D (2D): compute affinities \(q_{ij}\) using a Student-\(t\) distribution (heavier tails)
Minimize the KL divergence: \(\text{KL}(P \| Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log \frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}\)


The heavy-tailed \(t\)-distribution in low-D allows distant points to spread apart while keeping nearby points close → good cluster separation.

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
t-SNE: Key Parameters
Perplexity

Controls the effective number of neighbors
Typical range: 5–50
Low perplexity: fine local structure; high perplexity: more global structure

Iterations

t-SNE uses gradient descent; needs enough iterations to converge (typically 500–1000)

. . .
Cautions

Distances between clusters are not meaningful
Cluster sizes are not meaningful
Results depend on random initialization—run multiple times
Does not preserve global structure well

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
UMAP: Intuition
Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection
Based on topological data analysis concepts. Constructs a fuzzy simplicial complex (weighted graph) in high-D and optimizes a low-D layout to match.

Advantages over t-SNE

Better preservation of global structure (relative cluster positions)
Faster (scales better to large \(n\))
Theoretical grounding in Riemannian geometry and algebraic topology

Key parameters

n_neighbors: similar to perplexity. Controls local vs. global balance.
min_dist: controls how tightly points cluster in the embedding.

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
Comparing PCA, t-SNE, UMAP



Feature
