title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
section: The Phase Transition You Can’t Cross
text:
The Phase Transition You Can’t Cross
Baik, Ben Arous, and Péché (2005) proved a remarkable result: there is a sharp threshold below which PCA cannot detect a signal, no matter how much data you have.

For a signal-plus-noise model with \(p/n \to \gamma\), a signal eigenvalue \(\ell\) is detectable only if:
\[\ell > 1 + \sqrt{\gamma}\]


With \(p = 10{,}000\) and \(n = 100\) (\(\gamma = 100\)), you need signal strength \(> 11\) to detect a component. Weak signals literally merge with noise and become invisible.

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
A Practical Workflow
For high-dimensional biological data:

Start with PCA to get an overview, check for batch effects, choose number of informative dimensions
Apply t-SNE or UMAP on the top \(k\) PCs (not the raw \(p\)-dimensional data)
Never skip PCA—it denoises and speeds up nonlinear methods


Why PCA first?

Removes noise dimensions that confuse t-SNE/UMAP
Standard practice in single-cell analysis (Seurat, Scanpy both do this)
The “Johnson–Lindenstrauss” intuition: random projections preserve distances approximately, and PCA is even better than random

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
Cautions with Nonlinear Embeddings
Things that are NOT safe to conclude from a t-SNE or UMAP plot:

“These two clusters are far apart” (distances are distorted)
“This cluster is larger/denser” (sizes are distorted)
“There are exactly \(k\) clusters” (artifacts can create false clusters)
“The trajectory goes from A to B” (without additional evidence)



Use nonlinear embeddings for hypothesis generation, not hypothesis testing. Always validate findings with quantitative methods.

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
Connection to Factor Models
PCA is closely related to the factor model:
\[X = F L^T + E\]

\(F\) (\(n \times k\)): factor scores (one row per sample)
\(L\) (\(p \times k\)): factor loadings (one row per variable)
\(E\): noise


When \(E_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)\) (homoscedastic), PCA recovers the factors. When \(E\) has heterogeneous variance, probabilistic PCA or factor analysis may be more appropriate.
In genomics, surrogate variable analysis (SVA) uses this framework to estimate hidden confounders.

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
Discussion
Take a few minutes on each of these before we move to the lab.
Is 15% a lot?
You run PCA on \(p = 20{,}000\) genes measured on \(n = 100\) samples. PC1 explains 15% of the variance.

If all \(p\) variables were independent noise, each PC would explain about \(1/\min(n,p) = 1/100 = 1\%\).
PC1 explains 15× that. This is strong signal.

. . .
But: in the \(p \gg n\) regime, the Marchenko–Pastur law inflates eigenvalues—the leading eigenvalue of pure noise with \(\gamma = p/n = 200\) can be as large as \((1 + \sqrt{200})^2 \approx 229\) on the covariance matrix scale. Not all of that 15% is necessarily signal—some is inflated by high-dimensional noise. The scree plot and parallel analysis help you decide.

title: Lecture 3: Dimension Reduction
text:
Summary

PCA is the workhorse: fast, interpretable, optimal for linear structure. Always start here.
Scree plots and Marchenko–Pastur help decide how many components to keep.
t-SNE and UMAP reveal nonlinear structure but distort distances and sizes.
Best practice: PCA first, then nonlinear embedding on top PCs.
Nonlinear plots are for exploration, not confirmation.
In the \(p \gg n\) regime, random matrix theory tells us about the limits of what PCA can detect.

Up Next
Lab 3: Apply PCA and UMAP to the Golub dataset. Interpret biplots, scree plots, and embeddings.

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
Perfect Training, Catastrophic Prediction
A research team fitted ordinary least squares to predict patient outcomes from 500 gene expression features on 80 patients.


Training \(R^2\): 0.99 — the model fit the data almost perfectly
Test \(R^2\): \(-0.3\) — worse than simply predicting the mean for everyone



The model had memorized noise. With more features than samples, OLS finds a perfect interpolation — not because the biology is that clean, but because there are infinitely many solutions that pass through every data point.


This is the overfitting catastrophe that regularization prevents.

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
The Regression Problem in High-D
We want to model a response \(y \in \mathbb{R}^n\) as a function of predictors \(X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}\):
\[y = X\beta + \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2 I)\]

When \(p > n\):

OLS \(\hat{\beta} = (X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty\) does not exist (\(X^TX\) is singular)
Even when \(p < n\) but \(p\) is close to \(n\), OLS overfits: high variance, poor prediction
Need to regularize: trade some bias for reduced variance

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
Ridge Regression
Add an \(\ell_2\) penalty:
\[\hat{\beta}_{\text{ridge}} = \arg\min_\beta \left\{ \frac{1}{2n}\|y - X\beta\|_2^2 + \frac{\lambda}{2} \|\beta\|_2^2 \right\}\]

Closed-form solution: \(\hat{\beta}_{\text{ridge}} = (X^TX + n\lambda I)^{-1}X^Ty\)
(All three methods—ridge, LASSO, elastic net—use the glmnet convention with \(1/(2n)\) scaling on the loss, so \(\lambda\) is comparable across methods.)
Properties

Shrinks all coefficients toward zero (but none exactly to zero)
The penalty term makes the system invertible even when \(p > n\)
Equivalent to a Bayesian posterior with \(\beta_j \sim N(0, \tau^2)\) prior, where \(\lambda = \sigma^2/\tau^2\)

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
Ridge: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
As \(\lambda\) increases:

Bias increases: coefficients shrink toward zero
Variance decreases: estimates become more stable


There exists an optimal \(\lambda\) that minimizes prediction error. The mean squared error:
\[\text{MSE}(\hat{\beta}_{\text{ridge}}) = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance}\]
Ridge is optimal when many predictors have small effects (no true sparsity).

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
section: The Most Elegant Geometry in Modern Statistics
text:
The Most Elegant Geometry in Modern Statistics
Why does changing \(\|\beta\|_2^2\) to \(\|\beta\|_1\) — changing a sphere to a diamond — cause coefficients to become exactly zero?

The answer is corners.


The \(\ell_1\) constraint set (a diamond / cross-polytope) has corners on every coordinate axis. The \(\ell_2\) constraint set (a sphere) is perfectly smooth.
When the OLS contour ellipse expands until it touches the constraint set, it almost always hits a corner of the diamond — where one or more coordinates are exactly zero.


In \(p\) dimensions: the \(\ell_1\) ball has \(2p\) vertices, all on coordinate axes. High-dimensional geometry makes corner intersections even more likely.

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
The LASSO
Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (Tibshirani, 1996):
\[\hat{\beta}_{\text{lasso}} = \arg\min_\beta \left\{ \frac{1}{2n}\|y - X\beta\|_2^2 + \lambda \|\beta\|_1 \right\}\]

Key difference from ridge
The \(\ell_1\) penalty \(\|\beta\|_1 = \sum_j |\beta_j|\) produces sparse solutions: many \(\hat{\beta}_j = 0\) exactly.
LASSO simultaneously performs estimation and variable selection.

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
Geometry: Why \(\ell_1\) Gives Sparsity
The constraint region for \(\ell_1\) is a diamond (cross-polytope). The constraint for \(\ell_2\) is a sphere.
The OLS solution, projected onto the constraint set, is more likely to hit a corner of the diamond (where coordinates are zero) than the smooth surface of the sphere.

In \(p\) dimensions: the \(\ell_1\) ball has \(2p\) vertices (one per axis), making sparse intersections very likely.

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
LASSO: Solution Path
As \(\lambda\) decreases from \(\lambda_{\max}\) (where \(\hat{\beta} = 0\)) to 0:

Coefficients enter the model one at a time
The regularization path \(\hat{\beta}(\lambda)\) is piecewise linear (Efron et al., 2004, LARS)
Can compute the entire path efficiently

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
Choosing \(\lambda\): Cross-Validation
\(K\)-fold cross-validation:

Split data into \(K\) folds (typically \(K = 10\))
For each \(\lambda\): fit on \(K-1\) folds, predict on held-out fold
Average the prediction error across folds
Choose \(\lambda\) that minimizes CV error


Two common choices

\(\lambda_{\min}\): minimizes CV error (best prediction)
\(\lambda_{1\text{se}}\): largest \(\lambda\) within 1 SE of the minimum (more parsimonious model)

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
Elastic Net
Compromise between ridge and LASSO (Zou & Hastie, 2005):
\[\hat{\beta}_{\text{enet}} = \arg\min_\beta \left\{ \frac{1}{2n}\|y - X\beta\|_2^2 + \lambda\left[\alpha\|\beta\|_1 + \frac{1-\alpha}{2}\|\beta\|_2^2\right] \right\}\]

\(\alpha = 1\): LASSO
\(\alpha = 0\): Ridge
\(0 < \alpha < 1\): Elastic net


When to use elastic net: Correlated predictors (common in genomics). LASSO tends to arbitrarily select one from a group of correlated variables; elastic net selects the group.

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
Penalized Regression: Summary









Method
Penalty
Sparsity?
Best when…




Ridge
\(\frac{1}{2}\|\beta\|_2^2\)
No
Many small effects


LASSO
\(\|\beta\|_1\)
Yes
Few large effects


Elastic Net
\(\alpha\|\beta\|_1 + \frac{1-\alpha}{2}\|\beta\|_2^2\)
Yes
Correlated predictors




All are available in glmnet (R) and scikit-learn (Python).

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
Theoretical Guarantees
LASSO consistency (Zhao & Yu, 2006)
Under the irrepresentable condition and sufficient signal strength, LASSO recovers the true support (nonzero coefficients) with probability \(\to 1\) as \(n \to \infty\).
Prediction error bounds
Under restricted eigenvalue conditions, LASSO achieves prediction error:
\[\frac{1}{n}\|X\hat{\beta}_{\text{lasso}} - X\beta^*\|_2^2 = O\left(\frac{s \log p}{n}\right)\]
where \(s\) = number of true nonzero coefficients. This is near-optimal—you pay only \(\log p\), not \(p\).

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
MammaPrint: When Regularization Saves Lives
In 2002, Laura van ’t Veer measured 25,000 genes on 78 breast cancer patients and used a supervised method to find a 70-gene signature predicting metastasis (van ’t Veer et al., 2002, Nature).

The signature was commercialized as MammaPrint, cleared by the FDA, and validated in the MINDACT trial (Cardoso et al., 2016, NEJM): 6,693 patients. Women classified as high clinical risk but low genomic risk were randomized to chemotherapy vs. no chemotherapy.


Result: 94.7% five-year distant metastasis-free survival without chemo. 46% of women who would have received chemotherapy were spared — because a model built on 25,000 variables and 78 patients turned out to be right.

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
section: The Duke Fraud: When the Same Method Destroys Trust
text:
The Duke Fraud: When the Same Method Destroys Trust
At Duke University, Anil Potti used gene expression signatures to predict which chemotherapy drug a tumor would respond to. Clinical trials enrolled patients based on these signatures.

Two statisticians at MD Anderson — Keith Baggerly and Kevin Coombes — spent years trying to reproduce the analysis (Baggerly & Coombes, 2009, Ann. Appl. Stat.). They found mislabeled samples, off-by-one errors in gene indices, and results that were literally the opposite of what was claimed.


18 papers retracted. Potti’s medical license revoked. Patients had been assigned to treatments based on fraudulent models.

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
The Same Method, Different Outcomes
MammaPrint and the Duke fraud used the same method on the same kind of data for the same kind of question. The difference:


Validation: MammaPrint was tested in a 6,693-patient prospective randomized trial. Duke’s signatures were never properly validated.
Reproducibility: Baggerly and Coombes couldn’t reproduce the Duke analysis because the code and data were not shared. Van ’t Veer’s 70 genes replicated.
Cross-validation done correctly: The Duke group leaked information between training and test sets. MammaPrint did not.



The lesson: Regularization is a tool, not a guarantee. The model is only as good as the analysis pipeline.

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
LASSO Instability: A Warning
Even when the method is applied honestly, LASSO has a reproducibility challenge:

When predictors are correlated (as genes always are), LASSO arbitrarily picks one from each correlated group. Different samples → different arbitrary picks → different gene lists, same prediction accuracy.


This is why bootstrap stability analysis matters: it tells you which genes are consistently selected, not just selected once. Elastic net mitigates this via the grouping property.

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
section: Using Generative AI in High-Dimensional Analysis
text:
Using Generative AI in High-Dimensional Analysis
Large language models (Claude, ChatGPT, and others) can now write and debug analysis code, explain statistical output, suggest analysis strategies, and provide first-pass biological interpretations.

They are useful for code generation, syntax lookup, and boilerplate. They are unreliable for statistical judgment, numerical computation, and novel biological claims. Code may contain subtle bugs; gene function descriptions may be fabricated.

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
Common Errors to Watch For








Error
Why it matters
How you catch it




No multiple testing correction
Hundreds of false positives
Check for p.adjust() or equivalent


Uses theoretical null when empirical null is needed
Inflated false discovery rate
Look at the \(z\)-score histogram


Runs t-SNE on raw 20K dimensions
Slow, noisy, misleading
Check if PCA was applied first


Doesn’t set scale. = TRUE in PCA
Variables on different scales dominate
Read the code carefully


Uses lambda.min without justification
Overly complex model
Is the sparser lambda.1se model adequate?


Hallucinated gene functions
Wrong biological conclusions
Verify against GeneCards, NCBI Gene, PubMed

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
Verifying Biological Interpretations
After identifying a gene set, you might ask an AI to describe their functions. Always cross-reference the response:

Pick at least 3 genes
Look them up on GeneCards or NCBI Gene
Check: Did the AI get the function right? Did it invent a citation?

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
Other Modern Approaches (Brief Survey)
Deep learning for high-D biology

Autoencoders: Nonlinear dimension reduction; used in single-cell analysis (e.g., scVI)
Variational autoencoders (VAEs): Probabilistic; learn latent representations
Transformers: Protein structure prediction (AlphaFold), gene expression modeling (Geneformer)

. . .
When do these beat classical methods?

Very large \(n\) (thousands of samples/cells)
Complex nonlinear relationships
Transfer learning from pre-trained models

For \(n \leq\) a few hundred (typical in many BTP settings), penalized regression and empirical Bayes methods often perform comparably or better, and are much more interpretable.

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
Bringing It All Together
The complete high-dimensional analysis toolkit:








Day
Topic
Key Idea




1
Multiple testing & FDR
Control false discoveries when testing thousands of hypotheses


1
Empirical Bayes
Use the aggregate data to estimate prior, then individualize


2
PCA & UMAP
Find low-dimensional structure; visualize


2
Penalized regression
Predict and select variables when \(p \gg n\)


2
LLMs as tools
Accelerate coding and interpretation; verify everything

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
Discussion
Take a few minutes on each of these before we move to the lab.
Why not OLS?
You have \(n = 80\) samples and \(p = 5{,}000\) gene expression measurements. Why can’t you just run ordinary least squares?
. . .

Mathematical: \(X^TX\) is at most rank 80, so it’s singular—\((X^TX)^{-1}\) doesn’t exist.
Practical: Even if \(p\) were just slightly less than \(n\), OLS would overfit catastrophically—memorizing noise rather than learning signal.

title: Lecture 4: Penalized Regression & Modern AI Tools
text:
Summary

Ridge, LASSO, elastic net handle the \(p > n\) problem through regularization.
LASSO gives sparse solutions; ridge when you expect many small effects; elastic net for correlated predictors.
Cross-validation chooses the regularization strength.
LLMs are powerful tools for code generation and interpretation, but require critical evaluation.
Classical methods remain strong for moderate \(n\); deep learning shines with massive datasets.

Up Next
Lab 4: Penalized regression on the Golub dataset with biological interpretation.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text: In 2003, Avshalom Caspi and a team of researchers published a paper in Science that would become one of the most influential findings in psychiatric genetics. They reported that a variant in the serotonin transporter gene — a gene called 5-HTTLPR — predicted whether people who experienced stressful life events would develop depression. The idea was elegant: some people carry a version of this gene that produces less serotonin transporter protein, and those people, when exposed to stress, are more vulnerable to depression. Nature plus nurture, mediated by a single gene.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text: This is the key figure. The interaction looks clean — the short allele carriers show more depression under stress, the long allele carriers are protected. The paper was cited over 8,000 times. To put that in perspective, a typical well-cited paper in genetics gets maybe 200 citations. A famous one gets 1,000. 8,000 is a field-defining result. It launched an entire subfield. Hundreds of labs tried to replicate and extend the finding. Graduate students built dissertations around it. Textbooks cited it as a model of gene-environment interaction. If you took a psychology or genetics course between 2005 and 2015, you probably heard about it.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
8,000 citations

2003–2018: Hundreds of replication attempts
Some positive. Some negative. Some null.
Meta-analyses disagreed with each other.


Over the next fifteen years, hundreds of studies tried to replicate or extend the result. Some found the same thing. Some found the opposite. Some found nothing. Meta-analyses — studies that combine the results of many studies — disagreed with each other, depending on which studies they included and how they weighted them. This should have been a red flag. When a real effect exists, replications generally agree on its direction and approximate size. When studies disagree about whether an effect is positive, negative, or zero, that’s what noise looks like. But the field had reasons to keep believing. The biology was plausible — serotonin is genuinely involved in mood regulation. The original study was well-designed by the standards of its time.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
In 2019, a team led by Richard Border tested the 5-HTTLPR hypothesis — along with 17 other “candidate gene” hypotheses for depression — in samples ranging from 62,000 to 443,000 people. They had preregistered their analysis plan. They used the same measures and the same statistical approaches that the original studies had used, just with vastly more data.
They found nothing. Not “a smaller effect than expected.” Nothing. The serotonin transporter variant had no detectable relationship to depression, with or without stress. Neither did any of the other 17 candidate genes.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
This is the figure. Eighteen candidate genes. Zero replication. The entire candidate gene literature for depression was, in effect, noise.
How does a false positive survive for fifteen years? The short answer is: it wasn’t one false positive. It was thousands of them, across hundreds of studies, in a field that was running many statistical tests without accounting for how many tests it was running. And the tools to prevent this — to control the rate of false discovery when you’re testing many hypotheses — existed the entire time. The field just wasn’t using them.
That’s what this lecture is about. Not the serotonin gene specifically, but the general problem: what happens when you test many hypotheses, and what it looks like when you don’t account for it.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
How does a false positive survive for fifteen years?


Pause here. Let the question hang. Then: “We’re going to see this problem firsthand, using a real dataset that we’ll work with for the rest of the workshop. And we’ll start building the diagnostic tools to catch it.”

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text: In 1999, Todd Golub and colleagues published a landmark paper in Science showing that gene expression patterns alone could distinguish between two types of leukemia: acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) and acute myeloid leukemia (AML). Each patient’s tumor was profiled using a microarray — a technology that measures the expression level of thousands of genes simultaneously. In this case, 3,051 genes per patient after preprocessing. This is the dataset we’ll work with for the rest of the workshop.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
Patients
72


Genes
3,051


ALL
47


AML
25


Ratio p/n
~42






More variables than observations. 42× more.


The 72 patients fall into two groups: 47 with ALL and 25 with AML. These are different types of leukemia — they have different prognoses, different treatment responses, different biology. The question is: which genes are expressed differently between the two types?
This is a perfectly reasonable question. A clinician might want to know which genes distinguish the types so they can develop better diagnostics. A biologist might want to understand the molecular basis of the distinction.
So you do what anyone would do. You take each gene, one at a time, and ask: is this gene expressed at a different level in ALL patients than in AML patients? The standard tool is a t-test. You compute a test statistic, you get a p-value, and you decide whether the difference is “statistically significant.” Do this for all 3,051 genes and you get 3,051 p-values.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
\[3{,}051 \times 0.05 = 153\]



153 “significant” genes from pure noise.


Here’s the thing about p-values that everyone learns in their first statistics course and then immediately forgets the implications of: a p-value of 0.05 means that if the gene is not differentially expressed — if there is truly no difference between ALL and AML — there’s still a 5% chance of getting a test statistic this extreme or more extreme, just by chance. That’s fine for one test. You accept a 5% false positive rate as the cost of doing business.
But you’re not running one test. You’re running 3,051.
If none of the genes were actually different between ALL and AML — if the two types were molecularly identical, which they’re not, but bear with me — you’d still expect about 5% of your tests to come back significant at the 0.05 level. Five percent of 3,051 is 153. You’d get 153 “significant” results from pure noise.
The false positive rate is still 5%. The false positive count is 153. And if you’re a biologist looking at your results, you don’t see a rate — you see a list of genes. A long list. A list that looks like you’ve discovered something.
This is the multiple testing problem. It’s not subtle. The math is first-semester probability. And yet it fooled an entire field of psychiatric genetics for fifteen years.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
What “nothing” looks like










Here’s what you’d see if none of the genes were differentially expressed: a flat histogram. Uniform. Equal numbers of p-values in every bin. This is because, under the null hypothesis, p-values are uniformly distributed between 0 and 1. That’s not an approximation or an asymptotic result — it’s exact. Technically, this is true for continuous test statistics. Discrete test statistics produce p-values that are stochastically larger than uniform, which makes the test conservative. For gene expression data with continuous measurements, the uniform assumption is essentially exact. If nothing is going on, every bin gets the same number of p-values. Memorize this shape — it’s your baseline.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
The Golub data










Now consider the Golub data. The histogram is not flat. There is a pronounced spike near zero — hundreds of genes with very small p-values — and then a roughly flat region for the rest. The spike is signal: genes that are genuinely differentially expressed between ALL and AML. The flat part is the null genes — the genes where no real difference is present, producing the uniform distribution we would expect.
Don’t explain yet. Ask the audience: “What do you see?” Let them describe it — spike near zero, flat region, excess above the red line. Then confirm what they said.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
section: Three things this histogram tells you
text:
Three things this histogram tells you


Is there signal? Spike near zero → yes.
How much? The flat part estimates π₀ (fraction of null genes).
Is your analysis trustworthy? Flat part should be flat. If not, something is wrong.





Plot the histogram before you do anything else.



This one plot tells you three things. First, whether there’s signal. If the histogram is flat, there is nothing to detect. If there’s a spike near zero, something real is happening.
Second, roughly how much signal. The height of the flat part tells you the density of null p-values. If the flat part is at, say, 80% of the total, then roughly 80% of your genes are null and 20% are non-null. In the Golub data, the spike is substantial — ALL and AML are genuinely different diseases, and the transcriptomic differences are large.
Third, whether your analysis is trustworthy. A well-behaved analysis produces a histogram with a flat right side and a spike on the left. But sometimes the histogram looks wrong. If the bars increase toward the right — more large p-values than expected — your test is conservative. If there’s a bump in the middle, something is off with your test statistics. If the entire histogram is shifted left with no flat region, your null model is wrong. These pathological histograms are surprisingly common, and they tell you to stop and fix your analysis before interpreting any results.
I want you to internalize this: before you look at gene lists, before you compute adjusted p-values, before you run any correction procedure — plot the histogram. It takes ten seconds and it tells you whether the rest of your analysis is worth doing.
We’ll come back to this histogram in Lecture 2, where it turns from a diagnostic into an estimator. The flat part isn’t just telling you “these genes are null” — it’s telling you the proportion of null genes, and that proportion is the key ingredient in false discovery rate estimation. But I’m getting ahead of myself.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
[PLACEHOLDER — create a 2×2 panel showing: (1) well-behaved with signal, (2) all null/uniform, (3) anti-conservative (too many small p-values, inflated null), (4) conservative (dip near zero). Label each panel clearly.]
These are the shapes you’ll learn to recognize. Top left is what we just saw — signal plus a well-behaved null. Top right is all null — flat, no detectable signal. Bottom left is anti-conservative — the null is inflated, indicating a problem with the analysis, often batch effects or unaddressed confounders. Bottom right is conservative — the test is overly cautious, possibly because the test statistic is discrete. In every case, the histogram tells you what to do before you run any correction.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
Bonferroni correction

\[\frac{0.05}{3{,}051} \approx 1.6 \times 10^{-5}\]



Controls the family-wise error rate: P(any false positive) ≤ 0.05


The oldest and simplest answer is Bonferroni correction. The idea is almost embarrassingly straightforward: divide your significance level by the number of tests. Instead of calling a gene significant at p < 0.05, you call it significant at p < 0.05 / 3,051 ≈ 1.6 × 10⁻⁵.
The logic goes like this. You want the probability of making any false positive — even one — to be at most 5%. This is called the family-wise error rate, or FWER. If each individual test has a false positive probability of α/m, then by the union bound, the probability that at least one of the m tests produces a false positive is at most m × (α/m) = α. Done.
Bonferroni is clean, easy to explain, and requires no assumptions about the dependence structure of your tests. It works whether your genes are independent or correlated. It works whether you’re testing 10 hypotheses or 10 million. In genome-wide association studies, where you’re testing around a million genetic variants, the convention is a significance threshold of 5 × 10⁻⁸ — which is just Bonferroni at the million-variant scale. The actual number comes from a more sophisticated calculation involving linkage disequilibrium and the effective number of independent tests in the genome, but it’s in the Bonferroni ballpark.
And Bonferroni works, in the sense that it does what it promises. If you apply Bonferroni to 3,051 tests, the probability that any of your declared significant genes is a false positive is at most 5%.
But here’s the problem: Bonferroni controls the wrong thing.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
Where is the threshold?










Think about what the family-wise error rate actually means. It’s the probability of any false positive. Any. Even one. In a list of 500 significant genes, Bonferroni demands a guarantee that not a single one is a false discovery. That’s an extraordinarily high standard. And to achieve it, the threshold becomes so stringent that you miss most of the real signal.
You can barely see the red line. It’s jammed against zero. Only the very tip of the spike survives. Everything else — including real signal — gets thrown away.
If you’re an astronomer and you’re about to announce the discovery of gravitational waves, yes, you want to be very sure you haven’t made even one false positive. The standard there is “five sigma,” roughly one in 3.5 million. The cost of a false claim is enormous.
But if you’re a biologist screening 12,625 genes to find candidates for follow-up experiments, you’re in a completely different situation. You don’t need a guarantee that every gene on your list is real. You need a useful list — one where most of the genes are real and the false positives are a manageable fraction. If your list of 500 genes contains 25 false positives, that’s a 5% false discovery rate, and for most biological applications, that’s fine. You’ll validate the interesting ones in follow-up experiments anyway.
Bonferroni can’t give you that list. It’s trying to prevent a different kind of error — any error at all — and in doing so, it throws away most of your real discoveries.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
In 2009, a neuroscientist named Craig Bennett and his colleagues put a dead Atlantic salmon in an fMRI scanner. It was purchased at a local fish market. It was, by all accounts, very dead.
They showed the salmon a series of photographs of people in social situations and asked it — well, they ran the standard fMRI analysis pipeline — to determine the emotional valence of each photograph. The scanner measured blood oxygenation levels across thousands of tiny volume elements, called voxels, in the salmon’s body. For each voxel, a statistical test asked: does the signal in this voxel change in response to the photographs?
Without any correction for multiple comparisons, a cluster of voxels in the salmon’s brain cavity showed statistically significant “neural activity.” The dead fish appeared to be processing human emotions.
With a standard correction for multiple comparisons, the signal disappeared. Because of course it did — the salmon was dead.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
This is the figure. The colored blobs are “statistically significant” voxels in the dead salmon’s brain cavity. Without correction, the salmon is thinking about human emotions. With correction, it’s a dead fish.
Bennett presented this as a poster at a conference, and it became widely circulated, partly for its memorable framing and partly because it made a serious point: every fMRI analysis involves thousands of voxels, each tested independently, and the multiple testing problem is the same as in genomics. Bennett surveyed published fMRI studies and found that between 25% and 40% did not report correcting for multiple comparisons. The poster was memorable. The survey was concerning.
I’m telling you the salmon story because it’s memorable and because it makes the multiple testing problem feel visceral in a way that abstract calculations don’t. But the salmon was caught quickly — it was a deliberate demonstration. The next story is about what happens when false positives aren’t caught at all.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
The gap




Approach
Genes significant
Problem




No correction
Thousands
Hundreds of false positives


Bonferroni
A handful
Most real signal missed






You need a different question.


You need a different question. Not “did I make any false positives?” but “what fraction of my discoveries are false?” That question has an answer, and it’s the subject of Lecture 2. But before we get there, I want to show you what happens when people skip the correction entirely.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
Two questions

FWER (Bonferroni): Did I make any false positive?
\[P(\text{at least one false positive}) \leq \alpha\]
. . .
FDR (Benjamini-Hochberg): What fraction of my discoveries are false?
\[E\!\left[\frac{\text{false positives}}{\text{total discoveries}}\right] \leq \alpha\]


This is the conceptual reframe. Bonferroni asks: what’s the probability of any false positive? FDR asks: of the genes I called significant, what fraction are wrong? If I call 200 genes significant and the FDR is 10%, I expect about 20 of them to be wrong. The other 180 are real. That’s a useful list.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
Three approaches, one dataset










The Benjamini-Hochberg procedure finds the middle ground. We’ll learn how it works in Lecture 2. For now, just see the result: uncorrected gives you thousands with hundreds of false positives. Bonferroni gives you a handful. BH gives you a useful discovery set. Which list would you want for follow-up experiments?

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
How false positives survive

Publication bias Studies finding nothing sit in file drawers.
. . .
Winner’s curse To cross the significance threshold in a small sample, the observed effect has to overshoot the true value.
. . .
Garden of forking paths Many defensible analytical choices, each a branch point. The effective number of tests is much larger than reported.


How did the false positives survive? Three mechanisms, each reinforcing the others.
Publication bias. Studies that found a significant result for 5-HTTLPR were more likely to be published. Studies that found nothing were more likely to sit in a file drawer. The published literature was a biased sample of the evidence.
Winner’s curse. The studies that did get published — the ones that found significant results — necessarily overestimated the effect size. To cross the significance threshold with a small, noisy sample, the observed effect has to be larger than the true effect. So the early estimates of the 5-HTTLPR effect were inflated, and later studies with more realistic estimates looked like “failures to replicate.”
The garden of forking paths. There are many defensible choices in any analysis: which covariates to include, which subgroups to analyze, which outcome measure to use, how to define “stressful life events.” Each choice is a branch point, and the analysts — without any intent to deceive — could explore different branches until they found one that produced a significant result. This isn’t the same as running 12,625 explicit tests, but it has the same effect: the number of implicit tests is much larger than the number of reported tests.
None of this required bad intentions. The researchers who studied 5-HTTLPR were, for the most part, careful scientists who believed in their hypothesis and interpreted ambiguous evidence in its favor. That’s not fraud — that’s human cognition. The problem wasn’t the scientists. The problem was the statistical framework, which didn’t account for the scale of testing that was actually happening.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
Meanwhile, GWAS got it right





Candidate genes
GWAS




Tests per study
1–few
~1,000,000


Correction
Usually none
5 × 10⁻⁸


Depression loci found
0 (replicated)
100+






Same biology. Different statistical framework. Different results.


Meanwhile, a parallel approach had been yielding real results. Genome-wide association studies, which test hundreds of thousands or millions of genetic variants and apply stringent multiple testing correction — that 5 × 10⁻⁸ threshold — had been steadily finding genetic loci associated with depression. By 2019, GWAS had identified over 100 loci. These weren’t the same genes that the candidate gene studies had focused on. The two approaches — one without proper correction, one with — were studying the same biology and getting completely different answers.
The fix was known the entire time. GWAS adopted it from the beginning: test everything, correct for everything, accept the stringent thresholds. The candidate gene field tested one or a few genes per study, which seemed like it shouldn’t require correction — but across the entire literature, across hundreds of studies each making dozens of analytical choices, the effective number of tests was enormous. The correction should have happened at the field level, not just the study level.

title: The Gene That Wasn’t There
text:
The 5-HTTLPR story isn’t a story about bad scientists.
. . .
It’s a story about what happens when a field’s statistical framework doesn’t match the scale of its questions.
. . .
The framework exists.
That’s what we’ll build in Lecture 2.


The 5-HTTLPR story isn’t a story about bad scientists. It’s a story about what happens when a field’s statistical framework doesn’t match the scale of its questions. The framework to handle that scale exists — it’s what we’ll build in Lecture 2. But first, in the lab, you’re going to see it for yourself. You’ll take the Golub dataset, run those 3,051 tests, plot the histogram, apply Bonferroni, and feel the gap between “controls any error” and “gives you a useful answer.” That gap is where the false discovery rate lives.
